channel.title: APGO Journal
channel.description: World affairs, economics, technology, history, ideas and football analysis.

item.title: The Ceasefire Broke at Sea
item.description: Nine consecutive nights of renewed U.S. strikes, fresh damage to merchant vessels and thousands of stranded seafarers show why the Strait of Hormuz is now both a battlefield and a test of the maritime order.

item.title: Ankara’s Long-War Commitment
item.description: NATO has turned support for Ukraine into a two-year industrial pledge while expanding its eastern defences. The strategy is becoming more durable just as UN monitors record the worst monthly civilian toll since spring 2022.

item.title: The Debt Problem Is Now a Calendar
item.description: The world’s borrowing debate usually begins with how much is owed. In 2026, the sharper risk is when that debt matures, what rate replaces it and how much public policy gets crowded out during the rollover.

item.title: The AI Boom Has Reached the Substation
item.description: Data-center demand is growing faster than the power system built to serve it. The decisive questions are no longer confined to chips and models: they are about transformers, transmission, credible forecasts and who pays when a promised load fails to arrive.

item.title: The Declaration Was a Distribution System Before It Was a Sacred Object
item.description: Two hundred and fifty years after independence, the familiar parchment can obscure the more radical story: a text set in type overnight, carried by riders, reprinted by newspapers and made political by being read aloud.

item.title: The Sun Also Rises Turned Exile Into an Export
item.description: A century after publication, Hemingway’s first major novel still shapes how outsiders imagine Paris, Pamplona and expatriate life—even as the book itself is more wounded, exclusionary and skeptical than the lifestyle myth built around it.

item.title: One Goal, Two Stars: Spain Outlast Argentina to Reclaim the World Cup
item.description: Ferran Torres’s 106th-minute finish settled a final built on Spanish pressure and Argentine resistance. The 1–0 victory gave Spain a second men’s title—and completed an unusually concentrated run of international success.

item.title: Dostoevsky’s Underground Man Was Built for the Feed
item.description: A spiteful, self-conscious narrator from 1864 explains the inner machinery of posting: imaginary audiences, rehearsed humiliation and the freedom to choose what harms us.

item.title: The 48-Team World Cup Is Over. Its Scale Was the Story.
item.description: More countries reached the stage, more people entered the grounds and the scoring rate rose. The first expanded World Cup answered the simplest sporting objection—but left harder questions about format, travel and workload for a calmer audit.
